topic.what: Gibberish bias in BPE tokenization causes high-entropy secrets to become low token-entropy sequences prone to memorization
topic.how: Analyzes token-level vs char-level entropy of code secrets across CLLMs and discovers BPE distribution shift between training data and secrets
topic.why_matters: Exposes tokenizer-induced vulnerability where seemingly secure high-entropy secrets are actually easier to memorize and leak by LLMs

characteristics.what_it_measures: Information content of tokenized secret sequences
characteristics.context: 102 gibberish secrets vs non-secrets in training data memorization

characteristics.evidence: Table 2 - demonstrates human intuition vs LLM perception gap
characteristics.context: Secrets designed at char-level but LLMs process at token-level

characteristics.what_it_measures: Entropy normalized by outcome space size
characteristics.context: Indicates higher memorization likelihood per EM-Law

characteristics.what_it_measures: Distribution shift between secret data and training corpus
characteristics.evidence: Section 5 - Stack V2 vs secret token distributions
characteristics.context: Root cause of gibberish bias in BPE tokenization

characteristics.what_it_measures: Relative entropy degradation of secrets vs non-secrets by vocab size
characteristics.context: Ratio deviates from 1 with larger vocabulary

characteristics.what_it_measures: Proportion of perfectly memorized sequences that are secrets
characteristics.evidence: 102 of 847 zero-distance instances are gibberish secrets
characteristics.context: ACL 26 Findings paper - significant security risk

novelty: First identification of gibberish bias in CLLM tokenization
Formal characterization linking entropy-memorization law to secret leakage
Demonstration that larger vocabularies exacerbate secret leakage risk
Root cause analysis via BPE distribution shift (KL=2.668)
